title: Time Leverage

content.question: Where are you trading time 1:1 when you should be designing leverage?
content.business_lens: Content reused; systems run without you.
sop: Track your hours for one week and tag each as 1:1 (output stops when you stop) or leveraged (output continues without you).
Eliminate or delegate the lowest-value 1:1 tasks first.
Turn one recurring 1:1 task into something scalable, a recorded video, a template, a documented process.
Estimate your Output (dollars or units produced) against Time spent (hours) on this week's biggest deliverable (see calculation, page 5), and target the worst ratio first.
Reuse existing work (content, systems, training) instead of creating from scratch each time.
questions: Where are you trading time 1:1 when you should be designing leverage?
What did you do this week that, if recorded once, could be reused a hundred times?
How many hours this week produced output only while you were actively working, and stopped the moment you stopped?
content.equation_example: Output (units or dollars produced, count) divided by Time (hours spent, count) equals Leverage. Output $8,000 divided by Time 20 hours equals 400. Higher/rising means your time produces more without you adding more hours; lower/falling means output only grows if your hours do too.
content.closing_statement: Make your hours produce after you've stopped working.

caption:
Principle Nº 182: Time Leverage

Your time must multiply.

Ask yourself: Where are you trading time 1:1 when you should be designing leverage?
